scenario: A Node.js worker handles process-payment. The team wants the worker to retry transient HTTP errors automatically (e.g., 503) before reporting failure to Zeebe.
question: Where does retry-on-transient-error logic typically live in a Node SDK worker?

options.text: js SDK automatically retries all transient HTTP errors on behalf of workers without any configuration -- the default retry behaviour of the SDK varies by version and is not universally comprehensive. Workers must typically implement their own transient-error retry logic using HTTP client libraries with retry support or manual backoff loops.

BPMN retries re-activate the entire job from the beginning after the retry backoff period, which is less granular than worker-side retry that can distinguish error types, apply progressive backoff, and accumulate partial results across attempts without full job re-activation.

js workers -- this understates the retry architecture options. Multiple retry layers are available: worker-internal retry using HTTP client libraries (axios-retry, got, custom loops), BPMN-level retries on the Service Task element, and combinations of both. Each layer addresses different failure scenarios and they can be composed for layered resilience strategies.

Inside the worker's handler code -- implement retry logic using a library (axios-retry, got with retry, manual for loop with backoff). fail()). Combining with BPMN retries: BPMN's retries count applies on top -- if worker exhausts internal retries AND reports failure, BPMN retries can re-activate (each re-activation re-runs worker's internal retry loop). Be mindful of total retry count and time. 8).

a.text: Incorrect. Retry logic typically lives in the worker's handler code: HTTP libraries with retry support: axios-retry, got (built-in retry config), fetch with retry wrappers. Configure retry count, backoff, retry-on conditions. Manual loop: for i in 1..maxRetries: try call; if success return; if transient, wait + retry; if permanent, fail. Distinguish error types: transient (5xx, network errors, rate limiting) vs permanent (4xx, validation errors, business rule violations). Only retry transients. Example: `javascript async function processPayment(job) { for (let i = 1; i <= 3; i++) { try { const result = await callPaymentAPI(job.variables); return { paymentResult: result }; } catch (e) { if (isTransient(e) && i < 3) { await sleep(1000 * i); // Linear backoff continue; } else if (isPermanent(e)) { await job.error({ errorCode: "PAYMENT_REJECTED", errorMessage: e.message }); return; } else { throw e; // Let SDK / Zeebe fail the job } } } } Combine with BPMN retries: BPMN-level retries (e.g., retries = 3`) provide an additional safety net. If the worker exhausts its internal 3 retries and reports failure, BPMN's 3 retries kick in (each re-activates the worker, which retries internally 3 more times). Total: up to 9 attempts. Be aware of total time / load implications.
